text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
SURVEILLANCE VISIT NOTE

PATIENT: Denise Okafor
AGE: 50 years
ECOG PS: 0
Visit type: Established patient, routine oncology surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine surveillance visit, 24 months post bilateral mastectomy. No new complaints. Patient presents for ongoing follow-up in the context of prior stage IIB triple-negative breast cancer.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okafor is a 50-year-old woman of West African descent with a history of stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer of the left breast, diagnosed approximately two years ago. She presents today for her 24-month post-surgical surveillance visit. She is feeling well overall and has returned to full-time work as a high school biology teacher.

To briefly summarize her oncologic history: She was diagnosed with a 3.2 cm grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast upper outer quadrant, ER/PR/HER2 negative, Ki-67 85%, with one of three sentinel nodes positive. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT) was identified on paired tumor-normal NGS. Tumor PD-L1 CPS was 18 by 22C3, TILs 35%, and HRD score 68 (positive).

She received neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (Keytruda) 200 mg Q3W combined with dose-dense AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide Q2W x 4 cycles with pegfilgrastim) followed by weekly paclitaxel x 12 weeks with pembrolizumab per the KEYNOTE-522 regimen. Her neoadjuvant course was complicated by: (1) grade 2 immune-related thyroiditis during cycle 2 of pembro, requiring one cycle hold, symptomatic management with propranolol, and ultimately transition to levothyroxine replacement for hypothyroid phase; and (2) grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy during the paclitaxel phase, managed with paclitaxel dose reduction to 80% and initiation of duloxetine.

Post-neoadjuvant MRI demonstrated partial radiographic response with residual 1.6 cm enhancing mass and persistent axillary nodal involvement. Given BRCA2 carrier status and residual disease on imaging, she elected left modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection (levels I-II) and right prophylactic mastectomy, with bilateral immediate tissue expander reconstruction. Final surgical pathology showed residual invasive ductal carcinoma measuring 1.8 cm (ypT1c), 3 of 16 axillary nodes positive (ypN1a), RCB Class III — indicating extensive residual disease. Miller-Payne grade 3.

Adjuvant therapy included: capecitabine (Xeloda) 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 Q21 days x 8 cycles (dose reduced to 75% after grade 2 hand-foot syndrome during cycle 3), and continuation of pembrolizumab Q3W. Post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) was delivered to the left chest wall and regional nodal fields (50 Gy in 25 fractions with 10 Gy boost to chest wall, total 60 Gy), initiated 6 weeks post-surgery. Pembro was permanently discontinued after cycle 14 due to grade 3 immune-related hepatitis (ALT 412, AST 389, ~10x ULN), managed with high-dose steroids over an 8-week taper with full normalization of LFTs. Capecitabine was continued uninterrupted. All 8 cycles of cape were completed.

Following completion of all other adjuvant therapies and normalization of liver enzymes, she was started on adjuvant olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID per the OlympiA regimen, given her germline BRCA2 status and high-risk residual disease. She completed a full 52 weeks (one year) of olaparib without further dose reductions, though she did have grade 2 anemia at week 8 (Hgb 8.9 g/dL) that responded to a single dose of IV ferric carboxymaltose without requiring olaparib dose modification. Tissue expander exchange to permanent silicone implants (Mentor MemoryGel bilateral) was performed during the olaparib period without complications.

Surveillance imaging at 12 and 18 months post-surgery showed no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant disease. The 18-month scan incidentally identified a 4 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule; 3-month interval follow-up CT demonstrated stability, consistent with a benign etiology (granuloma or intrapulmonary lymph node). She has been seen by gynecologic oncology, and risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRBSO) is now scheduled for next month given her BRCA2 germline variant. She remains premenopausal with irregular menses following transient chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea.

Today, the patient reports no new breast or chest wall symptoms, no palpable masses, no bone pain, no shortness of breath, no new neurologic symptoms, and no abdominal pain. She denies cough, hemoptysis, or constitutional symptoms (night sweats, fevers, unintentional weight loss). She notes ongoing mild numbness and tingling in her fingertips and toes bilaterally at a baseline level, unchanged from prior visits. She feels these are tolerable and not interfering with daily activities. She is managing well emotionally and is engaged with a breast cancer support group.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, drenching night sweats, or significant unexplained weight loss. Reports stable weight.
MSK: No new bone pain, no joint swelling.
Neurologic: Grade 1 residual sensory peripheral neuropathy in hands and feet, stable and not worsening. No headaches, no focal weakness.
Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest or exertion, no cough, no hemoptysis.
GI: No nausea, no diarrhea, no abdominal pain. Denies melena or hematochezia. Appetite intact.
GU: Irregular menstrual cycles as previously noted. No dysuria or hematuria.
Skin: No rash, no new skin changes. Prior radiation dermatitis resolved. Hand-foot syndrome resolved.
Endocrine/thyroid: No palpitations, no heat intolerance, no cold intolerance. Tolerating levothyroxine well.
Psychosocial: Reports occasional anxiety related to surveillance imaging but overall coping well. Active in a peer support group.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
(Carried forward from established record)
1. Stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast — as above
2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.S1982Rfs*22)
3. Immune-related autoimmune thyroiditis secondary to pembrolizumab — now on levothyroxine replacement
4. Immune-related hepatitis grade 3 secondary to pembrolizumab — resolved, pembrolizumab permanently discontinued
5. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (grade 1 residual) — on duloxetine
6. Chemotherapy-induced transient amenorrhea — menstrual cycles returned, currently irregular
7. Post-surgical bilateral breast reconstruction with permanent silicone implants

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother: ovarian cancer at age 52
Maternal aunt: breast cancer at age 44
(Consistent with BRCA2 hereditary breast-ovarian cancer syndrome)

SOCIAL HISTORY
High school biology teacher, fully employed and active. Non-smoker. No alcohol or drug use noted. West African descent. BMI estimated 27-28 (clinically stable). Lives with partner. Engaged in regular light physical activity.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 88 mcg PO daily (dose recently increased from 75 mcg given TSH 5.1 at last visit)
2. Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily (for peripheral neuropathy)
3. Urea-based emollient cream PRN (feet and hands)
4. Vitamin D 1000 IU PO daily
5. No current systemic anticancer therapy

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 72, RR 14, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 99% on room air. Weight 168 lbs (BMI 27.4, stable).

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Mood euthymic. Well-nourished.

HEENT: Atraumatic, normocephalic. Oropharynx clear. No oral mucositis. No thyromegaly or thyroid nodule palpable. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Chest/Breast:
- Bilateral: Bilateral silicone implant-based reconstructions in place. Incision sites well healed bilaterally. No skin erythema, no warmth, no implant exposure, no wound breakdown.
- Left chest wall: Post-mastectomy and post-radiation changes present. Skin mildly indurated in the left chest wall, consistent with known radiation fibrosis. No palpable chest wall nodularity or mass concerning for local recurrence. No skin satellite lesions.
- Axillae: Left axilla: no palpable lymphadenopathy. Right axilla: no palpable lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly, no splenomegaly. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No lymphedema of the arms bilaterally. Fingernails and toenails intact, no pitting or onychodystrophy.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Sensation: mildly diminished light touch in fingertips and bilateral feet in stocking-glove distribution, symmetric, consistent with known grade 1 chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN). No ataxia. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
(Obtained at today's visit)

CBC:
- WBC 5.4 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hgb 12.1 g/dL (improved from nadir of 8.9 g/dL on olaparib; now within normal limits for premenopausal women)
- Platelets 214 x10^9/L (normal)
- MCV 92 fL (normal)

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- ALT 22 U/L (normal; fully recovered from irAE hepatitis)
- AST 19 U/L (normal)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L
- Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
- Electrolytes: within normal limits

Thyroid function:
- TSH 5.1 mIU/L (slightly above reference range 0.4-4.0 mIU/L at last check — levothyroxine dose already increased to 88 mcg; repeat TSH today pending)
- Free T4 0.9 ng/dL

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (18-month post-surgical surveillance): No evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease. Bilateral silicone implants intact. Left chest wall post-radiation soft tissue changes stable. 4 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule identified.

Follow-up CT chest (3-month interval for pulmonary nodule): 4 mm RLL pulmonary nodule stable, no growth. No new pulmonary nodules. Impression: Most likely benign (granuloma or intrapulmonary lymph node). No further workup indicated at this time.

Next scheduled surveillance CT: 30 months post-surgery (approximately 6 months from today).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER, STAGE IIB — 24-MONTH POST-SURGICAL SURVEILLANCE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE

Ms. Okafor continues to do exceptionally well at her 24-month surveillance visit. She has no clinical, laboratory, or imaging evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant disease. The previously identified 4 mm RLL pulmonary nodule has been confirmed stable on interval imaging and is not felt to represent metastatic disease — no further workup is planned for this finding at this time; it will be reassessed on the next routine surveillance CT. All adjuvant therapies have been completed (capecitabine x 8 cycles, olaparib x 52 weeks, pembrolizumab discontinued early due to irAE). She has received full-dose PMRT. At this point we are transitioning to scheduled surveillance only.

Plan:
- Continue surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 30 months post-surgery (~6 months from today)
- Bilateral breast implant surveillance as per plastic surgery recommendations
- Annual mammography of the chest wall/implant area is not routine post-mastectomy but patient was counseled; imaging surveillance will be CT-based per our protocol
- Return to clinic in 6 months or sooner for any concerning symptoms

2. GERMLINE BRCA2 VARIANT — RISK-REDUCING BILATERAL SALPINGO-OOPHORECTOMY SCHEDULED

Patient is actively engaged with gynecologic oncology. RRBSO is scheduled for next month. This is strongly recommended given her BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT), premenopausal status with irregular cycles (suggesting some residual ovarian function post-chemotherapy), and family history of ovarian cancer in her mother at age 52. We briefly discussed the implications of surgical menopause including vasomotor symptoms and bone health. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation is already underway. Baseline DEXA scan will be arranged in coordination with gynecologic oncology following the RRBSO. She is aware that hormone replacement therapy options post-RRBSO will need to be carefully discussed given her TNBC history (generally ER-negative TNBC does not carry the same theoretical concerns with very low-dose estrogen, but this will be deferred to gynecologic oncology and a shared discussion).

Plan:
- RRBSO next month with gynecologic oncology — patient confirmed scheduled
- Post-RRBSO follow-up: endocrine and cardiovascular risk assessment, DEXA scan
- Genetic counseling remains active for family members; patient has communicated variant to first-degree relatives per prior counseling

3. AUTOIMMUNE HYPOTHYROIDISM (PEMBROLIZUMAB-INDUCED) — LEVOTHYROXINE REPLACEMENT, DOSE RECENTLY UPTITRATED

Patient has been stable on levothyroxine since the hypothyroid phase of her immune-related thyroiditis approximately 18 months ago. Most recent TSH was 5.1 mIU/L (slightly above goal range), for which levothyroxine was already increased from 75 mcg to 88 mcg daily at the prior visit. Repeat TSH will be checked today; result pending.

Plan:
- Repeat TFTs in 6-8 weeks to confirm adequate suppression on 88 mcg dosing
- Continue levothyroxine indefinitely; expectation is this will be a permanent thyroid replacement requirement
- Endocrinology follow-up as needed

4. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — GRADE 1, STABLE

Residual grade 1 sensory peripheral neuropathy from paclitaxel, primarily affecting fingertips and bilateral feet in stocking-glove distribution. Patient tolerating well without functional impairment. Duloxetine 30 mg daily is providing adequate symptomatic relief. No progression noted.

Plan:
- Continue duloxetine 30 mg daily
- Reassess at next visit; if further improvement, may consider trial of duloxetine taper at some point in the future based on patient preference
- Physical therapy referral offered; patient declined at this time

5. SURVEILLANCE AND HEALTH MAINTENANCE

- Next oncology visit in 6 months (30-month post-surgery mark)
- Patient encouraged to maintain healthy BMI, regular physical activity — exercising regularly per report
- Primary care follow-up recommended for routine health maintenance (Pap smear, lipid panel, blood pressure monitoring, etc.), particularly given anticipated surgical menopause
- Patient briefed that standard surveillance does not include tumor markers (CA 27.29, CEA) as these are not felt to improve outcomes in breast cancer surveillance and are not part of routine monitoring
- Mental health: patient remains active in peer support group; continues to report manageable anxiety around surveillance. Resources offered including psychology referral; patient prefers current support group structure at this time

Overall, Ms. Okafor is doing remarkably well from an oncologic standpoint 24 months after definitive surgery. She has completed an intensive treatment course including neoadjuvant immunotherapy plus chemotherapy, bilateral mastectomy with reconstruction, PMRT, and extended adjuvant therapy with cape, pembro (early discontinuation), and olaparib. Her quality of life has substantially recovered. We continue to be vigilant given the high-risk features of her initial presentation (RCB Class III, node-positive, BRCA2 germline), but her current status is reassuring. Risks, benefits, and surveillance plan discussed in detail with patient; all questions addressed.

Time spent with patient: ~40 minutes
Diagnosis complexity: high

— Electronically signed, Medical Oncology
